enrichment.short_description: Mindustry is a free, open-source hybrid tower-defense and factory-automation game where players build resource-processing chains to arm defenses and conquer enemy bases.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source / Free (paid copies available on Steam and app stores)
enrichment.seo_title: Mindustry Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mindustry is a free open-source tower-defense and factory-building game. See features, platforms, pricing and how it compares to Factorio and similar games.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mindustry is a free and open-source real-time strategy game that blends tower defense with factory-style resource automation, created by a solo developer known by the pseudonym Anuke (real name reported as Anton Kramskoi, also credited under the handle Anuken). The project began in April 2017 as an entry for the itch.io 'Metal Monstrosity' game jam, where it placed first among 15 submissions. Its first public release (v1) shipped in 2017, and the game arrived on Steam with v3 in 2019. Mindustry is licensed under the GNU General Public License v3 (GPLv3), with its full source code hosted on GitHub under the Anuken/Mindustry repository, making it one of the more prominent fully open-source commercial-quality games. It is written primarily in Java using the libGDX framework, which allows it to run across Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS. Because it is open source, players can freely mod the game, inspect its code, and compile it themselves; the official builds are distributed free of charge from the developer's itch.io page and GitHub releases, while paid copies (identical in functionality) are sold on Steam and mobile app stores primarily to support development, since the game has no advertisements or in-app purchases. Core gameplay involves mining and transporting resources such as copper, lead, and titanium via conveyor belts, building factories that refine raw materials into components and ammunition, and constructing turrets and walls to repel waves of enemy forces, echoing mechanics popularized by Factorio combined with classic tower defense. Major updates have substantially expanded the game over time: version 6 (2020) introduced full real-time-strategy elements including unit commands and control groups, described by the community as a complete overhaul, while version 7 (2022) added a second planet, Erekir, along with new unit types and individually selectable RTS unit control. The game supports both single-player campaigns and multiplayer servers, including community-hosted and competitive PvP servers, and has an active modding community that produces custom maps, mods, and total conversions. Because there is no company behind Mindustry, there is no traditional pricing model, funding history, headquarters, or employee count; development is instead funded through voluntary donations, Patreon-style support, and optional paid Steam/app-store purchases. Its target audience is fans of automation and simulation games (Factorio, Satisfactory) who also enjoy tower-defense strategy, as well as players who value open-source, ad-free, DRM-free software. Its main points of differentiation from paid competitors like Factorio are its complete lack of monetization pressure, full source availability, cross-platform mobile support, and its GPL-licensed community-driven development model, which is unusual for a game of its scope and polish.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mindustry is a free, open-source game that combines factory automation with tower-defense combat. Players design conveyor-belt networks and processing plants to turn raw resources into ammunition and building materials, then use those materials to construct turrets and walls that fend off waves of enemies.

Created by a solo developer known as Anuke, Mindustry started life as a 2017 game jam entry and has since grown into a full-featured, actively updated title distributed on Steam, mobile app stores, itch.io, and GitHub. Its entire source code is released under the GPLv3 license, letting anyone inspect, modify, or redistribute the game.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core loop centers on resource logic: mining ores, routing them through conveyors and pipes, and refining them into usable materials, similar in spirit to Factorio but layered with active tower-defense combat against waves of AI enemies.

Later versions added real-time-strategy elements, letting players command mobile unit squads directly, plus a second in-game planet (Erekir) with distinct mechanics. The game supports single-player campaigns, cooperative and competitive multiplayer servers, custom map editors, and an active mod ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mindustry is entirely free to download from the developer's official GitHub releases and itch.io page, with no ads or in-app purchases in any version.

Identical copies are also sold on Steam and mobile app stores as an optional way to support the developer; buying there provides automatic updates and store integration but no additional features over the free build.

features.description: Build conveyor and pipe networks that mine, transport, and refine resources into ammunition and components.

features.description: Construct turrets and walls to defend bases against escalating waves of enemy forces.

features.description: Command mobile unit squads directly, introduced in version 6 for more active battlefield tactics.

features.title: Two-planet campaign
features.description: Play across Serpulo and Erekir, each with distinct resources, units, and mechanics.

features.title: Multiplayer servers
features.description: Join or host cooperative and competitive PvP servers with other players.

features.title: Full modding support
features.description: Create and install mods and custom maps thanks to the game's open codebase.

features.title: Cross-platform availability
features.description: Play on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS with cross-save support.

features.title: No ads or microtransactions
features.description: The game is completely free of advertising and in-app purchases in every distribution channel.

pros: Completely free and open source under GPLv3
No ads, no microtransactions, no predatory monetization
Deep, satisfying automation mechanics layered with active combat
Actively maintained with major free content updates
Available on nearly every platform including mobile
cons: Steep learning curve for players unfamiliar with automation games
Minimal narrative or story content
UI and visuals are functional rather than polished
No official customer support beyond community channels
Multiplayer server quality depends on community hosts

faqs.question: Is Mindustry really free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mindustry can be downloaded for free from the developer's GitHub and itch.io pages. Paid Steam and app-store versions are optional and offer no extra features.

faqs.question: Who created Mindustry?
faqs.answer: Mindustry was created by a solo developer known by the pseudonym Anuke, starting as a 2017 game jam entry.

faqs.question: What license is Mindustry released under?
faqs.answer: Mindustry's source code is licensed under the GNU General Public License v3 (GPLv3) and hosted publicly on GitHub.

faqs.question: What platforms does Mindustry support?
faqs.answer: Mindustry runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS.

faqs.question: Is Mindustry similar to Factorio?
faqs.answer: It shares automation and resource-processing mechanics with Factorio but adds tower-defense combat and RTS unit control as core gameplay pillars.

faqs.question: Can I mod Mindustry?
faqs.answer: Yes, the game has full modding support and an active community that creates custom maps, mods, and total conversions.

enrichment.short_description: Miniflux is a minimalist, self-hosted, open-source RSS and Atom feed reader built in Go with a focus on privacy, speed, and simplicity.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source / Freemium (free self-hosted, optional paid hosted plan)
enrichment.seo_title: Miniflux Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Miniflux is a minimalist, self-hosted RSS reader written in Go. Read about its features, self-hosting requirements, $15/year hosted plan, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Miniflux is a minimalist, opinionated, open-source RSS and Atom feed reader created by French developer Frederic Guillot. It is distributed under the permissive Apache 2.0 license, with its full source code (the current 'v2' rewrite) hosted publicly on GitHub under the miniflux organization. The application is written in Go and compiles to a single static binary with no external runtime dependencies other than a PostgreSQL database, which is the only supported data store. This architecture makes Miniflux extremely lightweight: a self-hosted instance can run comfortably on a low-cost VPS with roughly 1 vCPU and 1GB of RAM, often idling at around 20MB of RAM usage, and can be deployed via Docker, prebuilt binaries, or Debian/RPM packages. Miniflux has no dedicated company, VC funding, or headquarters behind it; it is maintained as an independent open-source project, and there is no publicly disclosed founding date for the project as a formal product beyond its GitHub commit history stretching back to the early-to-mid 2010s. Its design philosophy deliberately rejects feature bloat: the interface has no images loaded by default (for speed and privacy), no third-party trackers, no telemetry, and strips tracking parameters from article URLs. Core functionality includes fetching full article content from feeds that only publish summaries, keyboard-driven navigation, a clean reading view, support for external integrations (read-later services like Pocket, Wallabag, and Instapaper; notification services), OPML import/export, and a full REST API used by numerous third-party mobile and desktop client apps (since Miniflux itself ships only a web interface). Pricing is twofold: the software itself is completely free and open source for anyone to self-host, requiring only server costs (commonly cited as low as $5-10/month on a small VPS); alternatively, the maintainer offers an official hosted version at reader.miniflux.app for $15/year, which includes a 15-day free trial and removes the need to manage a server. This makes Miniflux dramatically cheaper than commercial hosted RSS readers such as Feedly or Inoreader, which typically charge $6-12/month for comparable premium tiers. Miniflux's target audience is developers, privacy-conscious power users, and technically capable individuals who want a fast, no-frills, self-hostable RSS reader rather than a feature-rich but heavier consumer product. Its main competitors include FreshRSS, Tiny Tiny RSS, NewsBlur, Feedly, and Inoreader; Miniflux differentiates itself through its extreme minimalism, single-binary Go implementation, strict privacy stance, and low resource footprint compared to PHP- or JavaScript-heavy alternatives like FreshRSS or Tiny Tiny RSS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Miniflux is an open-source, self-hosted RSS and Atom feed reader built around a philosophy of radical simplicity: 'the content is the most important thing, everything else is just noise.' It is written in Go by developer Frederic Guillot and released under the Apache 2.0 license.

Unlike feature-heavy readers, Miniflux ships as a single static binary with PostgreSQL as its only dependency, making it fast to deploy and cheap to run. Users who don't want to self-host can instead pay $15/year for the official hosted version.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Miniflux automatically fetches full article content for feeds that only publish summaries, strips tracking parameters and third-party trackers from links, and does not load images or telemetry by default, prioritizing privacy and speed.

It offers keyboard shortcuts for fast navigation, OPML import/export, integrations with read-later tools like Pocket and Wallabag, and a complete REST API that powers a wide ecosystem of third-party mobile and desktop apps.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Self-hosting Miniflux is completely free; users only pay for their own server, which can be a low-cost VPS given the software's minimal resource requirements (~20MB RAM idle).
For those who prefer not to manage infrastructure, Miniflux offers an official hosted plan at reader.miniflux.app for $15 per year, including a 15-day free trial.

features.title: PostgreSQL-only backend

features.title: REST API

features.title: Keyboard-driven UI
features.description: Extensive keyboard shortcuts enable fast, distraction-free reading.

features.title: Read-later integrations
features.description: Connects with services like Pocket, Wallabag, and Instapaper.

features.title: OPML import/export
features.description: Easily migrate feed subscriptions to and from other RSS readers.

pros: Extremely lightweight and fast, even on cheap VPS hardware
Fully open source under a permissive Apache 2.0 license
Strong privacy stance with zero telemetry and tracker stripping
Very low-cost hosted option ($15/year) as an alternative to self-hosting
Simple, distraction-free interface with strong keyboard support
cons: Minimalist UI lacks some power-user features found in FreshRSS or Tiny Tiny RSS
Requires PostgreSQL specifically, no alternative database support
Self-hosting requires basic server administration skills
No built-in mobile app; relies on third-party clients via its API
Limited visual customization compared to consumer readers like Feedly

faqs.question: Is Miniflux free?
faqs.answer: Yes, self-hosting Miniflux is completely free and open source. An optional official hosted version costs $15/year.

faqs.question: What database does Miniflux require?
faqs.answer: Miniflux requires PostgreSQL; it does not support MySQL, SQLite, or other databases.

faqs.question: Who created Miniflux?
faqs.answer: Miniflux was created by French developer Frederic Guillot and is maintained as an open-source project on GitHub.

faqs.question: Does Miniflux have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Miniflux itself provides only a web interface and REST API, but many third-party mobile apps support it via that API.

faqs.question: What license is Miniflux released under?
faqs.answer: Miniflux is released under the Apache 2.0 license.

faqs.question: How much server power does Miniflux need?
faqs.answer: Very little; a 1 vCPU, 1GB RAM VPS is generally sufficient for personal or small-team use, and the app idles around 20MB RAM.

use_cases.audience: Individuals seeking an affordable hosted RSS reader

enrichment.short_description: MinIO is a high-performance, S3-compatible object storage system available as an open-source Community Edition and a commercial AIStor Enterprise product.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Community Edition); Enterprise pricing starts around $96,000/year
enrichment.seo_title: MinIO Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MinIO is an S3-compatible object storage platform. Compare its free Community Edition vs. paid AIStor Enterprise pricing, features, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MinIO is a high-performance, software-defined, Kubernetes-native object storage system that is API-compatible with Amazon S3, allowing it to serve as a self-hosted or multi-cloud alternative to cloud object storage services. The company was founded in 2014 and is headquartered in Redwood City, California. MinIO raised a total of roughly $126 million in funding from investors including General Catalyst, Nexus Venture Partners, Intel Capital, Dell Technologies Capital, and SoftBank, reaching unicorn status with a $1 billion valuation after a $103 million Series B round in January 2022 led by Intel Capital. As of 2026 the company has roughly 200-210 employees. Architecturally, MinIO is written in Go and designed to run on commodity hardware, bare metal, virtual machines, containers, and Kubernetes, using erasure coding for data protection and supporting features like versioning, object locking, server-side encryption, replication, and lifecycle management, all exposed through an S3-compatible API that lets it act as a drop-in replacement for AWS S3 in many applications. MinIO's licensing history has been a major point of industry controversy: the project transitioned from the permissive Apache 2.0 license to the copyleft GNU AGPL v3 license between October 2019 and May 2021, a change many in the community felt was announced with little transparency and was designed to pressure large commercial users into paid licensing agreements; MinIO subsequently published public accusations against companies including Nutanix (2022) and Weka (2023) for alleged license violations. In March-May 2025, MinIO further stripped nearly all administrative features from the free Community Edition's web-based console (leaving only a basic object browser), pushing users toward the CLI (mc tool) or the paid AIStor Enterprise product for management functionality — a move that drew significant community backlash and spawned community forks such as OpenMaxIO aiming to preserve the pre-change functionality. Today MinIO's product is branded 'AIStor' and is positioned heavily toward AI/ML and GPU-inference storage workloads, alongside its traditional object storage use case. Pricing consists of a free, self-service Community Edition intended for developers and small single-node deployments; an 'Enterprise Lite' tier for smaller teams needing multi-node deployments with capacity up to 400 TiB and optional direct-to-engineer support; and a full Enterprise tier with 24/7/365 support and sub-4-hour SLAs, both of which require contacting sales, with the Enterprise tier reportedly starting around $96,000 per year according to industry reporting. MinIO's target audience includes enterprises and developers building AI/ML pipelines, data lakes, backup systems, and cloud-native applications who need S3-compatible storage without depending on a single public cloud vendor. Its main competitors include Ceph, SeaweedFS, Garage, Cloudian, and the S3 offerings of AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure; MinIO differentiates itself through its S3 API fidelity, Kubernetes-native design, and performance benchmarks, though its recent licensing and feature-removal decisions have pushed a portion of its open-source user base toward alternatives.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MinIO is a software-defined object storage system built to be fully compatible with the Amazon S3 API, letting organizations run S3-style storage on their own infrastructure, in Kubernetes clusters, or across multiple clouds. Founded in 2014 and headquartered in Redwood City, California, the company reached a $1 billion valuation after its 2022 Series B round.

MinIO offers a free, open-source Community Edition alongside a commercial 'AIStor' product line aimed at enterprises and AI/ML workloads. The project has drawn significant industry attention (and some controversy) for its licensing decisions, including a 2021 shift to AGPLv3 and a 2025 reduction of Community Edition management features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: MinIO provides full S3 API compatibility, erasure-coded data protection, object versioning and locking, server-side encryption, and multi-site replication, making it usable as a near drop-in replacement for cloud object storage in self-hosted or hybrid-cloud environments.

It is designed to be Kubernetes-native and runs on commodity hardware, and its more recent AIStor branding emphasizes high-throughput storage for AI training and inference pipelines, alongside traditional data lake and backup use cases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Community Edition is free and open source, intended for developers and single-node deployments, though as of 2025 its web console management features were significantly reduced in favor of the CLI.

Commercial tiers — Enterprise Lite and Enterprise — require contacting sales for pricing; Enterprise support is reported to start around $96,000 per year and includes 24/7 direct-to-engineer support with sub-4-hour SLAs.

features.title: S3-compatible API
features.description: Provides API compatibility with Amazon S3, enabling drop-in use with S3-native tools and SDKs.

features.title: Erasure coding
features.description: Protects data against drive and node failures using erasure-coded redundancy instead of simple replication.

features.title: Kubernetes-native design
features.description: Built to run natively in containerized and Kubernetes environments alongside bare-metal and VM deployments.

features.title: Object versioning and locking
features.description: Supports versioned objects and WORM-style object locking for compliance use cases.

features.title: Server-side encryption
features.description: Encrypts data at rest with support for multiple key management integrations.

features.title: Multi-site replication
features.description: Replicates data across sites or clouds for disaster recovery and multi-region access.

features.title: AIStor for AI/ML workloads
features.description: Enterprise product line optimized for high-throughput storage feeding GPU-based AI training and inference.

features.title: SUBNET support platform
features.description: Enterprise subscribers get health diagnostics, security reporting, and direct-to-engineer support via SUBNET.

pros: True S3 API compatibility for easy migration from cloud object storage
High performance on commodity hardware and Kubernetes
Erasure coding and replication provide strong data durability
Well suited to AI/ML and data-lake workloads at scale
Strong brand recognition as the leading open-source S3-compatible storage system
cons: AGPLv3 licensing creates compliance risk for some commercial users
Community Edition's web console lost most admin features in 2025
Enterprise pricing is opaque and reportedly expensive (~$96,000/year+)
Aggressive public license-enforcement actions have damaged community trust
CLI-first management in the free tier raises the learning curve for non-technical admins

faqs.question: Is MinIO free to use?
faqs.answer: The Community Edition is free and open source, though as of 2025 its web console lost most administrative features, which now require the CLI or a paid Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: What license does MinIO use?
faqs.answer: MinIO is licensed under the GNU AGPL v3, a copyleft license it adopted between 2019 and 2021, replacing its earlier Apache 2.0 license.

faqs.question: Is MinIO compatible with Amazon S3?
faqs.answer: Yes, MinIO implements the S3 API, allowing many S3-native tools, SDKs, and applications to work with it with minimal changes.

faqs.question: How much does MinIO Enterprise cost?
faqs.answer: MinIO does not publish enterprise pricing; industry reporting indicates plans starting around $96,000 per year, with exact pricing requiring a sales quote.

faqs.question: Who founded MinIO and when?
faqs.answer: MinIO was founded in 2014 and is headquartered in Redwood City, California.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to MinIO?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Ceph, SeaweedFS, Garage, Cloudian, and cloud-native object storage from AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure.

use_cases.title: AI/ML data infrastructure
use_cases.description: Enterprises building GPU-based AI training and inference pipelines that need high-throughput, S3-compatible storage.

use_cases.title: Multi-cloud and hybrid storage
use_cases.description: Organizations wanting S3-compatible storage that runs identically on-premises and across multiple public clouds.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted backup and data lakes
use_cases.description: Teams that need cost-effective, self-managed object storage for backups, logs, or data-lake workloads.

features: Basic object browser UI (admin features removed in 2025)
Lite SUBNET subscription with health diagnostics
Full multi-node architecture at any scale
Dedicated onboarding and team governance tools

enrichment.short_description: MintHCM is a free, open-source Human Capital Management (HCM) platform forked from SuiteCRM, covering recruitment, onboarding, performance, and workforce management.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source (free) with paid implementation/support services
enrichment.seo_title: MintHCM Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MintHCM is a free, open-source HR and HCM platform built on SuiteCRM. Explore its recruitment, onboarding, and performance features plus pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MintHCM is an open-source Human Capital Management (HCM) platform designed to manage the full employee lifecycle, including recruitment, employer branding, onboarding, employee profiles, skills tracking, performance evaluation, time and leave management, and offboarding. It is architecturally a fork of SuiteCRM (itself derived from SugarCRM Community Edition), repurposed and substantially reworked from a sales-CRM codebase into an HR-focused system, meaning its underlying API and data model share strong similarities with SuiteCRM. MintHCM officially launched in May 2018, with its first public open-source release following in 2019; the Poland-based IT consultancy eVolpe has partnered with the project since that first public release and provides commercial implementation, customization, and support services through minthcm.com, while the core software itself remains free. The project is licensed as fully open source (AGPL v3, per its own positioning as having 'no gated features'), meaning organizations can download, self-host, and modify the platform at no licensing cost, paying only for their own server infrastructure or for optional professional services. As of recent tracking, the project has accumulated tens of thousands of downloads and several hundred GitHub stars, with community support available via forums, Discord, and documentation wikis rather than a traditional enterprise support contract structure (though paid support is available through eVolpe and partners). MintHCM has been translated into multiple languages, including Polish and Czech, reflecting its origins and core community base in Central/Eastern Europe. More recently, MintHCM has repositioned itself around AI-driven HR workflows, describing itself as an 'AI-enabled open source HCM' with native support for MCP (Model Context Protocol) and A2A (agent-to-agent) architectures, positioning the platform for organizations wanting to build AI agents on top of their HR data without vendor lock-in, and emphasizing full ownership of data, code, and infrastructure with deployment options on-prem, in the cloud, hybrid, or at the edge. A notable functional limitation is that MintHCM does not include payroll processing, which must be handled via separate integrated systems. Its target audience includes small and mid-sized organizations, staffing agencies, and HR teams seeking a fully open-source, self-hostable, and customizable HR platform as an alternative to closed-source SaaS HR systems like BambooHR, Workday, or Rippling. MintHCM's key differentiators are its zero-licensing-cost open-source model, deep customizability inherited from its SuiteCRM/SugarCRM lineage, full data ownership, and its more recent focus on AI-agent-native HR tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MintHCM is a free, open-source Human Capital Management platform built as a fork of SuiteCRM, adapted specifically for HR use cases rather than sales pipelines. It covers the full employee lifecycle from recruitment and onboarding through performance management and offboarding.

Officially launched in May 2018 and open sourced publicly in 2019, MintHCM has been developed with support from the Poland-based consultancy eVolpe, which also offers paid implementation and customization services on top of the free core platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform covers recruitment and employer branding, employee onboarding and offboarding, skills and performance management, time and leave tracking, workforce analytics, and emerging capabilities like gamification and mobile access.

Because it is fully open source and self-hostable, organizations retain complete control over their HR data and can customize workflows, fields, and integrations extensively, inheriting the flexible architecture of its SuiteCRM/SugarCRM lineage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: MintHCM itself is completely free to download, self-host, and modify, with no licensing fees for any features under its open-source license.

Organizations that want professional implementation, hosting, customization, or support can purchase paid services from eVolpe and other partners, but this is optional and separate from the free core software.

features.description: Self-hosted deployment (on-prem, cloud, hybrid, or edge) with no vendor lock-in.

features.title: Multilingual support
features.description: Available in multiple languages including English, Polish, and Czech.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing fees
Full data ownership via self-hosting, no vendor lock-in
Highly customizable thanks to SuiteCRM/SugarCRM-derived architecture
Covers the full HR lifecycle from recruitment to offboarding
Active AI-agent-native direction (MCP/A2A) for modern HR automation
cons: No built-in payroll processing; requires separate integration
Smaller community and ecosystem than mainstream commercial HR platforms
Requires self-hosting and technical setup, unlike turnkey SaaS HR tools
Support relies on community channels or paid third-party services
Documentation and polish can lag behind well-funded commercial competitors

faqs.question: Is MintHCM free?
faqs.answer: Yes, MintHCM is fully open source and free to download, self-host, and modify, with no licensing fees.

faqs.question: Is MintHCM related to SuiteCRM?
faqs.answer: Yes, MintHCM is a fork of SuiteCRM (itself derived from SugarCRM Community Edition), reworked from a sales CRM into an HR-focused platform.

faqs.question: Does MintHCM handle payroll?
faqs.answer: No, MintHCM focuses on soft-HR functions and does not include payroll management, which must be integrated separately.

faqs.question: When was MintHCM launched?
faqs.answer: MintHCM officially launched in May 2018, with its first public open-source release in 2019.

faqs.question: Who supports MintHCM?
faqs.answer: The project is community-maintained, with the Poland-based consultancy eVolpe providing paid implementation and support services since the first public release.

faqs.question: What languages does MintHCM support?
faqs.answer: MintHCM supports multiple languages, including English, Polish, and Czech.

enrichment.short_description: Mintlify is a modern documentation platform that helps developer teams build, host, and maintain API references and product docs, used by companies like Anthropic and Cursor.
enrichment.seo_title: Mintlify Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Mintlify is a developer documentation platform backed by a16z. Compare its Starter, Pro, and Enterprise pricing, features, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Mintlify is a documentation platform built to help developer-facing companies create, host, and maintain API references and product documentation. It was founded in 2022 by Han Wang and Hahnbee Lee, who met at Cornell University and built the company around the shared frustration of writing and maintaining software documentation. Mintlify is headquartered in San Francisco and has raised approximately $21 million in total funding: a $2.8 million seed round in May 2022 led by Bain Capital Ventures (with participation from TwentyTwo Ventures and Sourcegraph co-founder Quinn Slack), followed by an $18 million Series A in September 2024 led by Andreessen Horowitz (a16z), with participation from Bain Capital Ventures, Y Combinator, and TwentyTwo Ventures. As of the Series A announcement, Mintlify powered documentation for thousands of companies, including notable AI and developer-tool companies such as Anthropic, Perplexity, and Cursor, and reported reaching over 20 million developers annually. Functionally, Mintlify lets teams write documentation in Markdown/MDX, then automatically generates a fast, searchable, SEO-friendly documentation website with built-in components, API playgrounds, and versioning. The platform has increasingly incorporated AI features, including an AI-powered writing assistant, automated content generation from OpenAPI specs and codebases, and an in-docs AI chat 'Assistant' that answers user questions directly from the documentation content, plus AI 'Agent' and 'Automations' capabilities for keeping docs in sync with product and code changes. It also exposes documentation as an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server, letting AI coding assistants and agents query docs programmatically. Pricing follows a three-tier freemium model: a free Starter plan for individuals and small teams offering full platform access, a custom domain, a web editor, authentication, an MCP server, an API playground, and 10,000 usage credits per month (with $0.01 per overage credit); a paid Pro plan for startups and growing teams that adds the AI Agent, Assistant, Automations, preview deployments, admin APIs, and advanced insights, with custom credit volumes and discounts; and a custom-priced Enterprise plan for larger organizations that adds SSO, SCIM, RBAC, a performance SLA, enterprise security and legal terms, self-hosting options, migration support, and 24/7 incident monitoring. Mintlify's target audience is developer-tool and API-first companies — from early-stage startups to large enterprises and AI labs — that need polished, maintainable documentation without building custom tooling in-house. Its main competitors include GitBook, ReadMe, Docusaurus (self-hosted/open source), and Stripe-style custom docs stacks; Mintlify differentiates itself through its design-forward output, deep AI integration (writing assistance, in-docs chat, MCP server exposure), and strong adoption among prominent AI and developer-tool companies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Mintlify is a documentation platform that helps developer-focused companies write, host, and maintain polished API references and product docs from Markdown/MDX source files. Founded in 2022 by Cornell graduates Han Wang and Hahnbee Lee, the company is headquartered in San Francisco.

The company has raised roughly $21 million, including an $18 million Series A led by Andreessen Horowitz in September 2024, and its customers include prominent developer-tool and AI companies such as Anthropic, Perplexity, and Cursor.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Mintlify converts Markdown/MDX content into a fast, searchable, SEO-optimized documentation site, complete with API playgrounds, versioning, and prebuilt UI components for common documentation patterns.

The platform has layered in significant AI functionality, including an AI writing Assistant embedded in docs for end users, an Agent and Automations to keep documentation synced with code and API changes, and native exposure of docs as an MCP server for AI coding tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Mintlify offers a free Starter plan with full platform access, a custom domain, authentication, and 10,000 monthly usage credits, suitable for individuals and small teams.
The Pro plan adds AI Agent, Assistant, and Automations features for growing teams, while Enterprise pricing is custom and adds SSO/SCIM/RBAC, SLAs, self-hosting options, and dedicated support.

faqs.answer: Yes, Mintlify offers a free Starter plan with full platform access, a custom domain, and 10,000 monthly usage credits.

faqs.question: Who founded Mintlify?
faqs.answer: Mintlify was founded in 2022 by Han Wang and Hahnbee Lee, who met at Cornell University.

faqs.question: How much funding has Mintlify raised?
faqs.answer: Mintlify has raised approximately $21 million total, including an $18 million Series A led by Andreessen Horowitz in September 2024.

faqs.question: What companies use Mintlify?
faqs.answer: Mintlify is used by companies including Anthropic, Perplexity, and Cursor, reportedly reaching over 20 million developers annually.

faqs.question: Does Mintlify support AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, Mintlify includes an AI writing Assistant, in-docs AI chat, automations to keep docs updated, and MCP server exposure for AI coding tools.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Mintlify?
faqs.answer: Self-hosting is available as part of the Enterprise plan.

enrichment.short_description: Minus Games is a small open-source suite of applications for distributing, running, and syncing PC games and save files from a self-hosted home server to client devices.
enrichment.seo_title: Minus Games Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Minus Games is an open-source home-server game and save-file distribution suite. Learn how it works, its features, and free self-hosted pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Minus Games is a niche, open-source software suite for distributing PC games and their save files from a self-hosted server to client machines, aimed primarily at home-server and homelab users who want to manage a personal game library outside of commercial storefronts like Steam. The project is maintained on GitHub under the account Accessory (repository Accessory/minus_games), and its documentation is published as a user guide at accessory.github.io/minus_games_user_guide. Public information about the project's founding date, creator identity, company affiliation, and funding is minimal or unavailable, consistent with its status as a small, community-maintained open-source tool rather than a commercial product. The suite consists of five separate applications: a Minus Games Server that hosts the game library, a Minus Games Client and a GUI client that download, install, and launch games on end-user machines, a Minus Games Finder that scans a designated folder on the server to detect installed games (with support for common engines such as Unity), and a Minus Games Updater that keeps game installations current with the latest files from the server. A key differentiating feature is save-file synchronization: in addition to keeping game installation files current across devices, Minus Games also syncs save files, allowing a player to start a session on one machine and continue seamlessly on another. The typical setup workflow involves configuring the server, placing game files in a designated directory, triggering a scan via the finder's REST endpoint, and then allowing client devices to browse, download, and launch games from that server. The project is distributed for free as open-source software, with no commercial pricing tiers; it is also packaged for Linux via Flathub for easier installation of the GUI client. Because of its narrow scope and small community, Minus Games has limited public documentation of company-style details (headquarters, employee count, funding), and users should expect it to function more like a personal/hobbyist infrastructure tool than a polished commercial product. Its target audience is technically inclined home-server operators and PC gamers with a library of non-Steam or DRM-free games who want Steam-like remote installation and save synchronization across their own devices. Comparable tools in this space include Playnite, Lutris, Heroic Games Launcher, and GameLauncher, though Minus Games' specific focus on a client-server home-network architecture with save-sync sets it apart from library aggregators that primarily unify existing storefronts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Minus Games is an open-source suite for distributing games and save files from a self-hosted server to client devices, built for home-server and homelab environments rather than as a commercial product.

The project is maintained on GitHub by the account Accessory, with documentation available as a user guide. It targets users who want Steam-like remote game installation and save synchronization for their own DRM-free or non-Steam game libraries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The suite is made up of five components: a server that hosts the library, a client and GUI for downloading and launching games, a finder that scans for installed games (including Unity-engine titles), and an updater that keeps files current.

Its standout feature is save-file synchronization alongside game-file syncing, letting players continue their progress seamlessly when switching between devices connected to the same Minus Games server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Minus Games is free, open-source software with no commercial pricing tiers; users only incur the cost of running their own home server hardware.
The GUI client is also distributed via Flathub for straightforward installation on Linux systems.

No commercial support or SLA

faqs.question: Is Minus Games free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Minus Games is completely free and open source.

faqs.question: What does Minus Games do?
faqs.answer: It distributes, installs, and syncs games and save files from a self-hosted server to client devices, similar to a personal Steam-like system.

faqs.question: Does Minus Games sync save files?
faqs.answer: Yes, syncing save files across devices is one of its core features, alongside syncing game installation files.

faqs.question: Where can I install Minus Games?
faqs.answer: The GUI client is available via Flathub for Linux, with server and client components available on GitHub.

faqs.question: Who created Minus Games?
faqs.answer: The project is maintained on GitHub under the account Accessory; detailed public information about the creator is limited.

faqs.question: Does Minus Games require Steam?
faqs.answer: No, it is designed for distributing non-Steam or DRM-free games from your own server.

enrichment.short_description: Miro is a cloud-based online whiteboard and visual collaboration platform used by teams for brainstorming, agile planning, diagramming, and workshops.
enrichment.starting_price: Free; paid plans from $8/month per member
enrichment.headquarters: Amsterdam, Netherlands and San Francisco, California, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Miro Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Miro is a leading online whiteboard platform valued at $17.5 billion. Compare its Free, Starter, Business, and Enterprise pricing plans and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Miro is a cloud-based visual collaboration platform, commonly described as an online whiteboard, used by distributed teams for brainstorming, diagramming, agile planning, product design, and workshops. The company was founded in 2011 by Russian entrepreneurs Andrey Khusid and Oleg Shardin under the original name RealtimeBoard; it rebranded to Miro in 2019 (a name inspired by artist Joan Miro) to reflect an expanded vision of end-to-end visual collaboration beyond a simple whiteboard tool. Miro is jointly headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands and San Francisco, California, with additional offices including Austin and Sydney, and employs roughly 1,800-2,000 people as of the mid-2020s. The company has raised approximately $476 million in total funding, including a $25 million Series A in 2018 led by Accel and a landmark $400 million Series C in January 2022 co-led by ICONIQ Growth, Accel, Atlassian, Dragoneer, GIC, and Scale Venture Partners, which pushed Miro's valuation to $17.5 billion. Miro has made strategic acquisitions to expand its product, including video-conferencing startup Around (2022) and AI design tool company Uizard (2024), and has increasingly positioned itself as an 'innovation workspace' rather than a pure whiteboarding tool, addressing fragmented cross-team workflows. As of 2025-2026, Miro serves over 90 million registered users across more than 250,000 organizations worldwide, and the company remains private with no confirmed IPO date, though industry commentary has speculated about a future public offering given its scale and valuation. Functionally, Miro provides an infinite canvas for sticky notes, diagrams, flowcharts, mind maps, and freeform drawing, combined with real-time multi-user collaboration, video chat, and asynchronous commenting. The platform includes over 7,000 templates, more than 4,700 shapes and diagramming elements for technical use cases, data tables, interactive prototyping tools, and AI-powered features (AI-generated diagrams, summarization, and workflow automation) accessed via a credit-based system. It integrates with more than 250 third-party tools, including two-way integrations with Jira, Asana, and Linear, and offers an MCP (Model Context Protocol) interface for AI agent access. Pricing follows a freemium model: a Free plan offers 3 editable boards and limited AI access; a Starter plan at $8/month per member (billed annually) adds unlimited private boards and more AI credits; a Business plan at $20/month per member (billed annually) adds unlimited workspaces, unlimited guests, and AI workflows/agents; and a custom-priced Enterprise plan (minimum 30 members) adds enterprise-grade security, SCIM provisioning, regional data hosting, and dedicated account support. Miro's target audience spans agile software teams, product designers, UX researchers, consultants, and enterprise innovation teams. Its main competitors include Figma (FigJam), Microsoft Whiteboard, Lucidchart/Lucidspark, and Mural; Miro differentiates itself through the breadth of its template and integration ecosystem, its scale (90M+ users), and its evolution from a niche whiteboard into a broader visual workspace platform with AI-driven features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Miro is a cloud-based visual collaboration platform built around an infinite digital whiteboard, used by teams for brainstorming, diagramming, agile planning, and remote workshops. Founded in 2011 as RealtimeBoard by Andrey Khusid and Oleg Shardin, the company rebranded to Miro in 2019 and is now jointly headquartered in Amsterdam and San Francisco.

Backed by $476 million in funding and valued at $17.5 billion after its January 2022 Series C round, Miro serves over 90 million users across more than 250,000 organizations, and has expanded well beyond whiteboarding into a broader 'innovation workspace' through acquisitions like Around and Uizard.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Miro provides an infinite canvas supporting sticky notes, diagrams, flowcharts, mind maps, and freeform drawing, with real-time multi-user editing, video chat, and commenting for synchronous and asynchronous collaboration.

The platform includes over 7,000 templates, thousands of technical diagramming shapes, data tables, interactive prototyping, AI-powered generation and summarization tools, and integrations with more than 250 third-party apps including Jira, Asana, and Linear.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Miro offers a Free plan with 3 editable boards, a Starter plan at $8/month per member for unlimited private boards, and a Business plan at $20/month per member adding unlimited workspaces and AI workflows, all billed annually for the listed rates.
An Enterprise plan with custom pricing (minimum 30 members) adds enterprise security, SCIM provisioning, and dedicated account support for large organizations.

features.title: MCP support for AI agents
features.description: Exposes board data via Model Context Protocol for AI agent workflows.

pros: Extremely large template and integration ecosystem
Real-time collaboration works well for distributed and hybrid teams
Scales from free individual use to large enterprise deployments
Continuously expanding AI features for diagram generation and summarization
Strong brand trust with 90M+ users and 250,000+ organizations
cons: Paid plans get expensive quickly for larger teams (per-member pricing)
AI features are credit-limited even on paid plans
Can feel overwhelming for users who only need simple diagramming
Enterprise plan requires a 30-member minimum
Some advanced features require Business or Enterprise tiers

faqs.question: Is Miro free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Miro offers a Free plan with 3 editable boards and limited AI access; paid plans start at $8/month per member.

faqs.question: Who founded Miro and when?
faqs.answer: Miro was founded in 2011 by Andrey Khusid and Oleg Shardin under the name RealtimeBoard, rebranding to Miro in 2019.

faqs.question: How much is Miro worth?
faqs.answer: Miro was valued at $17.5 billion following its $400 million Series C funding round in January 2022.

faqs.question: Where is Miro headquartered?
faqs.answer: Miro is jointly headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands and San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: What is Miro used for?
faqs.answer: Miro is used for brainstorming, agile planning, diagramming, product design, and remote or hybrid team workshops.

faqs.question: What are Miro's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Key competitors include Figma's FigJam, Microsoft Whiteboard, Lucidspark, and Mural.

enrichment.short_description: MiroTalk is a self-hosted, open-source suite of WebRTC video-calling platforms (P2P, SFU, and more) built by independent developer Miroslav Pejic, unrelated to the Miro whiteboard company.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source (free) with optional commercial licenses and paid support
languages: and 130+ other languages via built-in localization
enrichment.seo_title: MiroTalk Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: MiroTalk is an open-source, self-hosted WebRTC video calling platform with P2P and SFU editions. See features, AGPLv3 pricing, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: MiroTalk is a family of self-hosted, open-source WebRTC video-communication platforms created and maintained by independent developer Miroslav Pejic (GitHub handle miroslavpejic85). It is important to note that MiroTalk is entirely unrelated to Miro, the venture-backed online whiteboard company headquartered in Amsterdam and San Francisco — the similar naming is coincidental, and MiroTalk has no corporate, ownership, or investment ties to Miro. MiroTalk's codebase is released under the GNU Affero General Public License v3.0 (AGPLv3), meaning it is free to self-host and modify, with the requirement that modifications distributed to others remain open source under the same license. The project consists of several distinct sub-products, each maintained as a separate GitHub repository: MiroTalk P2P (peer-to-peer architecture for fast, direct browser-to-browser calls, hosted publicly as a live demo at p2p.mirotalk.com), MiroTalk SFU (a Selective Forwarding Unit architecture built with mediasoup, positioned as a self-hosted alternative to Zoom for larger group calls, demoed at sfu.mirotalk.com), MiroTalk C2C (a simplified one-to-one cam-to-cam calling variant limited to two participants per room for maximum privacy), MiroTalk BRO (a live broadcasting variant for streaming to unlimited viewers), and MiroTalk WEB (a room scheduling and management dashboard). The flagship MiroTalk P2P repository has accumulated around 4,600+ GitHub stars and roughly 750 forks, with an active Discord community and thousands of commits, indicating a moderately popular niche open-source project rather than a large company. Technically, the platforms are built primarily in JavaScript/Node.js with HTML and CSS, deployable via a small number of Docker Compose or npm commands, and can run on personal servers, VPS instances, or cloud infrastructure. Feature-wise, MiroTalk supports video calls up to 8K resolution at 60fps, screen sharing, session recording, markdown-enabled chat, a collaborative whiteboard, file sharing, OIDC-based authentication, JWT credentials, host protection and room passwords, a full REST API, embeddable iframe widgets, and localization into over 130 languages. There is no dedicated company behind MiroTalk, no formal funding rounds, headquarters, or employee count; the project instead sustains itself through GitHub Sponsors, corporate sponsorships listed on its repositories, and the sale of optional commercial/extended licenses through the developer's CodeCanyon marketplace listing for organizations that want additional customization rights or support beyond the free AGPLv3 terms. Its target audience is developers, startups, and privacy-conscious organizations that want to self-host video conferencing rather than depend on third-party cloud video platforms, avoiding recurring per-seat SaaS fees and retaining full control over call data. Its main competitors and comparison points include Jitsi Meet (also open source and self-hostable), BigBlueButton, Zoom, and Google Meet; MiroTalk differentiates itself through its lightweight self-hosting footprint, multiple architecture options (P2P vs. SFU) for different scale needs, and a permissive-to-use-but-copyleft AGPLv3 license that keeps the core product free.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: MiroTalk is a suite of open-source, self-hosted WebRTC video-calling platforms created by independent developer Miroslav Pejic. It is not affiliated with Miro, the whiteboard company — the naming similarity is coincidental.

The project includes multiple variants for different needs: MiroTalk P2P for direct peer-to-peer calls, MiroTalk SFU (built on mediasoup) for larger group meetings as a Zoom alternative, plus C2C, BRO, and WEB variants for cam-to-cam calls, broadcasting, and meeting scheduling respectively.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: MiroTalk supports video calls up to 8K at 60fps, screen sharing, session recording, markdown chat, a collaborative whiteboard, and file sharing, all deployable on a self-hosted server via Docker or Node.js.

Security and access-control features include OIDC authentication, JWT credentials, host protection, and room passwords, alongside a full REST API and embeddable widgets for integrating video calling into other applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: MiroTalk's core software is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license, allowing anyone to self-host and modify it at no cost, provided modifications distributed to others remain open source.

The developer also offers optional commercial licenses via CodeCanyon and accepts sponsorships through GitHub Sponsors for organizations wanting additional support or usage rights beyond the AGPLv3 terms.

features.title: Peer-to-peer video calling
features.description: MiroTalk P2P connects browsers directly for fast, low-latency calls without routing through a media server.

features.title: SFU architecture for group calls
features.description: MiroTalk SFU uses a Selective Forwarding Unit (via mediasoup) to scale video conferencing to larger groups.

features.title: Up to 8K/60fps video
features.description: Supports high-resolution video calling alongside screen sharing and session recording.

features.title: Collaborative whiteboard and chat
features.description: Built-in markdown-enabled chat and a shared whiteboard for real-time collaboration during calls.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Deployable in a handful of commands via Docker Compose or Node.js on any personal server or VPS.

features.title: OIDC and JWT authentication
features.description: Supports OpenID Connect login, JWT credentials, room passwords, and host protection controls.

features.title: Full REST API
features.description: A complete API enables programmatic room creation and integration into third-party applications.

features.title: Extensive localization
features.description: The interface is translated into over 130 languages.

pros: Free and fully open source under AGPLv3
No per-seat SaaS fees since it is self-hosted
Multiple architecture options (P2P and SFU) for different scale needs
Rich feature set including 8K video, recording, and whiteboard
Active community with thousands of GitHub stars and an open Discord
cons: Requires self-hosting and server management, no managed SaaS offering from the creator
AGPLv3 license requires derivative works to remain open source
Maintained primarily by a single independent developer, not a company
No formal SLA or enterprise support without a separate commercial license
Easy to confuse with the unrelated Miro whiteboard company due to naming similarity

faqs.question: Is MiroTalk related to Miro the whiteboard company?
faqs.answer: No. MiroTalk is an independent open-source project by developer Miroslav Pejic, entirely unrelated to Miro, the venture-backed whiteboard company.

faqs.question: Is MiroTalk free?
faqs.answer: Yes, MiroTalk is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license for self-hosting; optional commercial licenses are available separately.

faqs.question: What is the difference between MiroTalk P2P and SFU?
faqs.answer: MiroTalk P2P connects users directly for small calls, while MiroTalk SFU uses a media server (mediasoup) to efficiently scale to larger group meetings.

faqs.question: Who created MiroTalk?
faqs.answer: MiroTalk was created and is maintained by independent developer Miroslav Pejic.

faqs.question: Can I try MiroTalk without installing it?
faqs.answer: Yes, live demo instances are available at p2p.mirotalk.com and sfu.mirotalk.com.

faqs.question: What license does MiroTalk use?
faqs.answer: MiroTalk is released under the GNU Affero General Public License v3.0 (AGPLv3).

use_cases.title: Cost-conscious teams avoiding per-seat fees

features: Community support via GitHub and Discord
